stem: Let \( u(x,y) = ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])y^{2} + ([[C]])xy + ([[D]])x \) and \( v(x,y) = ([[G]])x^{2} + ([[H]])y^{2} + ([[K]])xy + ([[M]])y \). The Cauchy–Riemann equations require \( u_{x} = v_{y} \) at every point where a function \( f=u+iv \) is (complex-)differentiable. Evaluate \( u_{x} - v_{y} \) at \( (x,y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \).
solution: Compute \( u_{x} = 2([[A]])x + ([[C]])y + ([[D]]) \) and \( v_{y} = 2([[H]])y + ([[K]])x + ([[M]]) \), then substitute \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) into each and subtract: \( u_{x} - v_{y} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes \( u_{y} + v_{x} \) (the OTHER Cauchy–Riemann quantity) instead of \( u_{x} - v_{y} \).
This adds \( u_x \) and \( v_y \) instead of subtracting them.
This has the subtraction backwards, computing \( v_{y} - u_{x} \) instead of \( u_{x} - v_{y} \).
The value is not always \( 0 \); it is only \( 0 \) when the Cauchy–Riemann equation \( u_x=v_y \) happens to hold at this point.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( u(x,y) = ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])y^{2} + ([[C]])xy + ([[E]])y \) and \( v(x,y) = ([[G]])x^{2} + ([[H]])y^{2} + ([[K]])xy + ([[L]])x \). The Cauchy–Riemann equations require \( u_{y} = -v_{x} \) at every point where a function \( f=u+iv \) is (complex-)differentiable. Evaluate \( u_{y} + v_{x} \) at \( (x,y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \).
solution: Compute \( u_{y} = ([[C]])x + 2([[B]])y + ([[E]]) \) and \( v_{x} = 2([[G]])x + ([[K]])y + ([[L]]) \), then substitute \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) into each and add: \( u_{y} + v_{x} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes \( u_{x} - v_{y} \) (the OTHER Cauchy–Riemann quantity) instead of \( u_{y} + v_{x} \).
This subtracts \( v_x \) from \( u_y \) instead of adding them.
This has the subtraction backwards, computing \( v_{x} - u_{y} \) instead of \( u_{y} + v_{x} \).
The value is not always \( 0 \); it is only \( 0 \) when the Cauchy–Riemann equation \( u_y=-v_x \) happens to hold at this point.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( u(x,y) = ([[P]])e^{x}\cos y \) and \( v(x,y) = ([[Q]])e^{x}\sin y \). Evaluate \( u_{x} - v_{y} \) at \( (x,y) = \left([[x0]], \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{6}\right) \).
solution: Compute \( u_{x} = ([[P]])e^{x}\cos y \) and \( v_{y} = ([[Q]])e^{x}\cos y \), substitute the point, and subtract: \( u_{x} - v_{y} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes \( u_{y} + v_{x} \) (the OTHER Cauchy–Riemann quantity) instead of \( u_{x} - v_{y} \).
This adds \( u_x \) and \( v_y \) instead of subtracting them.
The value is \( 0 \) only when \( P=Q \) or \( \cos y_{0}=0 \); in general it need not vanish.
This has the wrong sign from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: For the analytic function \( f(z) = z^{3} + ([[c]])z \), write \( f(x+iy) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y) \), so that \( u(x,y) = x^{3} - 3xy^{2} + ([[c]])x \) and \( v(x,y) = 3x^{2}y - y^{3} + ([[c]])y \). Evaluate \( u_{x} - v_{y} \) at \( (x,y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \).
solution: Since \( f(z)=z^{3}+([[c]])z \) is analytic everywhere, the Cauchy–Riemann equations \( u_x=v_y \) hold at every point, so \( u_{x}-v_{y}=[[answer]] \) regardless of \( (x_0,y_0) \). Indeed \( u_{x} = 3x^{2}-3y^{2}+([[c]]) \) and \( v_{y} = 3x^{2}-3y^{2}+([[c]]) \) are identical expressions.
distractor_rationales: This adds \( u_x \) and \( v_y \) instead of subtracting them.
This mistakenly treats only the \( x^{3} \) term's derivative, ignoring the rest of \( u_x \) and all of \( v_y \).
This mistakenly treats only the \( -y^{3} \) term's derivative, ignoring the rest of \( v_y \) and all of \( u_x \).
This is the shared linear coefficient alone, not \( u_x - v_y \).
A function being analytic does not make this difference equal to \( 1 \); it is always exactly \( 0 \).
A function being analytic does not make this difference equal to \( -1 \); it is always exactly \( 0 \).
